title: The Presidential Suite

jokes.text: Moira wearing a blindfold for Johnny's ribbon-cutting ceremony at a motel
jokes.setup: Johnny is making a grand ceremony out of opening a new budget motel location
jokes.punchline: The pomp and circumstance is wildly disproportionate to the humble occasion — a motel ribbon-cutting with blindfolds
jokes.explanation: The visual of Moira in a blindfold for a budget motel ribbon-cutting perfectly encapsulates the Roses' fish-out-of-water grandeur applied to mundane situations. Character-specific and earned.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Do you have proper scissors? I need to cut this ribbon.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I have scissors.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Those are cuticle scissors, Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: They're multi-purpose. A woman of my stature needs to maintain her cuticles at all times. One never knows when one will be called upon to shake hands with someone important.

jokes.text: "What are these? These won't cut a ribbon." "They're cuticle shears, John. Do you expect hedge clippers to be drawn from my purse?"
jokes.setup: Johnny asks Moira for scissors to cut the ribbon
jokes.punchline: Moira produces cuticle shears and is genuinely indignant at the expectation that she'd carry anything more industrial
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The phrase 'drawn from my purse' is perfectly Moira — theatrical, grand, and completely self-unaware. The indignation at being expected to carry practical tools is pure character comedy. Only Moira Rose could deliver this. Justifies the 8+ score.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Taut? Is that like an old-timey word or something?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, Roland, it means tight or tense.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh. I thought you were speaking in tongues.

jokes.text: "I'm sorry, taut? What is that, old English? How about I hold it tight?"
jokes.setup: Johnny instructs Roland to hold the ribbon 'taut'
jokes.punchline: Roland pretends 'taut' is archaic vocabulary and offers 'tight' as a translation
jokes.explanation: A solid Roland moment — his deliberate anti-intellectualism is well-established character comedy. Slightly predictable but executed with good timing and specificity ('old English' is a nice touch).

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: At this rate, we'll be cutting this ribbon until next fall.

jokes.text: "How long is this gonna take, Johnny, should I cancel my New Year's plans?"
jokes.setup: Johnny is struggling to cut the ribbon with cuticle shears
jokes.punchline: Roland sarcastically implies the ribbon-cutting is taking so long it might carry into the new year
jokes.explanation: Competent impatience gag from Roland. The New Year's reference is a fun specificity but the joke is fairly conventional. Functional.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I think it's a perfectly taut observation, Roland.

jokes.text: After all the ceremony, step two turns out to be 'deep cleaning the rooms'
jokes.setup: After the grandiose ribbon-cutting ceremony with all its pomp, Johnny announces 'onto step two'
jokes.punchline: Stevie deflates the grandeur entirely: 'Deep cleaning the rooms.' A completely mundane, unglamorous next step.
jokes.explanation: A nice structural joke — the entire opening ceremony was built up as something important, and it punctures into the most unglamorous reality imaginable. Stevie's dry delivery is key. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I want the photographer to capture a very specific aesthetic. Something that evokes the image of wealthy people fainting.

jokes.text: "I'd like to be put to use. What's step three?" / "I think I need to lie down, that blindfold was quite taut."
jokes.setup: Moira just expressed wanting to help, then immediately invents a reason to rest
jokes.punchline: She claims exhaustion from wearing the blindfold — using the word 'taut' in a callback to Roland's earlier complaint — as an excuse to avoid work
jokes.explanation: This is a lovely layered joke: Moira's instant laziness is classic character comedy, but recycling 'taut' (the word Roland just mocked) to describe the strain of wearing a blindfold is a tight callback that rewards attentive viewers. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: I have thousands of them in storage.

jokes.text: David's wedding photo aesthetic description: "Annie Leibowitz for Vanity Fair... two very rich people that have just woken up after fainting on a dusty old couch."
jokes.setup: Ray the photographer shows David and Patrick cheesy, theme-park-style backdrop options for their engagement shoot
jokes.punchline: David's alternative vision is hyper-specific, pretentious, and simultaneously self-aware — 'after fainting on a dusty old couch' is such a precise absurdist image
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's standout line. The specificity of 'after fainting on a dusty old couch' is brilliantly absurd — it's not just 'elegant,' it has a story. It's pure David Rose: aspirational, theatrical, and completely ridiculous. Only he would describe his ideal photo this way. Easily justifies 8+ scores.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, you drive me to the spa, and then you wait in the car while I get pampered. So technically, you're getting the breeze through the window. Very refreshing.

jokes.text: "As maid of honour, I pick the second volcano."
jokes.setup: Ray is showing terrible backdrop options; David is horrified
jokes.punchline: Stevie, as maid of honour, weighs in with a completely straight-faced preference for one of the volcanoes
jokes.explanation: Stevie's dry, deadpan engagement with the volcano options is perfectly in character. 'As maid of honour, I pick the second volcano' is delivered with the solemnity of a real creative decision. The contrast between Stevie's neutrality and David's horror is the comic engine here.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's a light sun exposure as part of the spa treatment.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Light sun? David, that's just tanning.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's not tanning, Patrick. It's a controlled light sun experience.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You're describing a tanning bed.

jokes.text: "Okay, you are hanging onto that job by a thread."
jokes.setup: Stevie endorses the volcano backdrop as maid of honour
jokes.punchline: David threatens her maid of honour status with complete seriousness
jokes.explanation: A good David line — the idea that a maid of honour can be fired for having wrong aesthetic opinions is very David Rose. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick needs a spray tan because he looks absolutely anemic next to my Mediterranean complexion.

jokes.text: "I don't know if you've thought about wedding favours, but might I suggest mouse pads, I got thousands downstairs."
jokes.setup: David and Patrick are discussing the aesthetic vision for their engagement shoot
jokes.punchline: Ray pivots to pitching mouse pads as wedding favours, revealing he has thousands in stock — a completely out-of-left-field non-sequitur
jokes.explanation: Ray is a minor recurring character whose entrepreneurial randomness is a reliable gag. 'Thousands downstairs' is the escalation that makes it funny — he's not just suggesting one, he's clearly trying to unload inventory. Good economy and character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Ray Butani
dialogue.line: Well, I've been thinking about our mousepad designs, and I wanted to present a summer collection. However, there's an arbitrary seasonal restriction on an underwater theme.

jokes.text: David describes the spa gift as "a relaxing five-minute scalp massage followed by some light sun"
jokes.setup: David explains the spa gift he's giving Patrick
jokes.punchline: The gift is comically minimal — five minutes of scalp massage and 'some light sun' (a spray tan) — described as a lavish treat
jokes.explanation: The euphemism of 'light sun' for spray tan and the microscopic 'five-minute' qualifier make David's self-framing as generous funny. Competent but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so the airline just gives you bad milk? Like, they force you to drink it?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: No, no, I chose to drink it.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Why would you choose to drink airline milk?

jokes.text: "Do I get pampered?" "No, you get to drive him to the spa..."
jokes.setup: David describes Patrick's pampered spa gift
jokes.punchline: Stevie asks if she gets pampered too; David tells her she's the chauffeur
jokes.explanation: A reliable Stevie/David dynamic beat — her hope dashed, his obliviousness intact. Functional.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I'm sorry about the gift. I know it's generic.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, Ted, listen to this. 'To someone special, may your travels be filled with wonder and joy.' That's... that's about me.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: It says that on like a thousand of those.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, it's speaking to me. I feel very seen right now.

jokes.text: Stevie receives Patrick's spa appointment — she gets a spray tan on David's credit card without asking
jokes.setup: David says he's sending Patrick to get a scalp massage as a pre-wedding gift
jokes.punchline: Stevie casually reveals she already took the appointment and charged the spray tan to David's credit card
jokes.explanation: Stevie's deadpan self-interest at David's expense is a reliable dynamic. 'Okay well that's fraud and you're a criminal' is David at his best. The beat is economical and the character pairing earns the laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I think we can just drop it. No need to milk this topic any further.

jokes.text: "Okay, well that's fraud and you're a criminal, so where even is my life partner?"
jokes.setup: Stevie admits to charging her spray tan to David's credit card
jokes.punchline: David calls it fraud but immediately moves on, making the accusation feel like a throwaway, then the phrase 'life partner' adds an extra layer of David's particular vernacular
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'fraud' to 'you're a criminal' said with total breezy nonchalance before pivoting to find Patrick is peak David Rose. Economy is excellent — three beats of comedy in one breath.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm imagining myself in a luxurious Shanghai hotel suite, soaking in an enormous marble bathtub overlooking the Huangpu River, and suddenly, a pod of orcas surfaces right before me. I'm there in my silk robe, champagne flute in hand, watching these magnificent creatures breach, and thinking, 'This is authentic travel.'

jokes.text: Stevie's spray tan — she walks in visibly bronzed, David immediately notices
jokes.setup: David gave Patrick a spa appointment; Stevie stole it
jokes.punchline: Stevie walks in visibly tanned, the visual proof of her crime landing before a word is said
jokes.explanation: The visual payoff of Stevie appearing bronzed is the punchline to the setup established moments before. The 'Why do you look...? Hot?' exchange is a tight, well-structured visual gag.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, of course Johnny has business ethics. He learned from the best. I once produced a film called 'Tort Reform,' and the legal implications were absolutely exquisite.

jokes.text: "Why do you look...?" "Hot?"
jokes.setup: David sees Stevie's spray tan and is at a loss for words
jokes.punchline: Stevie fills in 'hot' for him with complete self-satisfaction
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — the joke is two words and lands entirely on the confidence of the delivery. Stevie's self-assured interruption is character-perfect. The gap between what David means and what Stevie supplies is the laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, there's this scene where the detective has to go undercover at a massage parlor, and she has to take off her top to maintain her cover. And then later, there's another scene where she's in the police station and her shirt gets ripped during a struggle, and you see her bra. And then in the third act, she falls into a river and her clothes get all wet and see-through.

jokes.text: Patrick and David look at the test photo together; Patrick sees it's not that bad, but David walks it back: "Okay, well, the lighting is not good so. I mean..."
jokes.setup: David claims the photo looks bad because of him standing next to Patrick
jokes.punchline: When Patrick says 'It's not that bad,' David immediately qualifies with poor lighting — revealing he knows his argument was weak
jokes.explanation: David's backtrack is a small but charming moment of self-awareness undermining his earlier manipulation. Good character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, Johnny, standing on that moral high ground must be giving you vertigo, because the view from up there is absolutely dizzying. I'm getting nauseous just watching you perch on that pedestal of righteousness.

jokes.text: "Side by side, my Mediterranean complexion makes you look a little anemic, so the spray will just even it out."
jokes.setup: David and Patrick take a test photo together
jokes.punchline: David explains that Patrick looks 'anemic' next to his own complexion, framing a self-compliment as concern for Patrick's appearance
jokes.explanation: 'Mediterranean complexion' is a very David Rose self-description — elevated, slightly pretentious, and delivered as objective fact. The selfishness disguised as concern is character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Photographer
dialogue.line: What is all this?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's a draped fabric installation. It's very chic, very editorial.

jokes.text: The mouse pad Jocelyn made for the school computer lab with 'Under the Sea' theme — shown as a sample wedding favour
jokes.setup: Ray insists on pitching mouse pads as wedding favours
jokes.punchline: The sample he produces is a school-made 'Under the Sea' seasonal mouse pad — the most inappropriate possible wedding favour
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the mouse pad gag — first we hear about them, then we see the specific awfulness of Jocelyn's school-made seasonal example — is a good slow-burn visual payoff. 'Only available during winter months' is an excellent absurdist detail.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You put a spray tan on my credit card?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Do you understand what you've done? You've committed fraud. You're a criminal.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It was like forty dollars.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Forty dollars?! That's forty dollars more than I expected to see charged to my account for your tanning needs!

jokes.text: Ted arrives with an airport gift that reads 'Have a great flight'
jokes.setup: Ted arrives to surprise Alexis, apologizing for limited airport gift shop options
jokes.punchline: The gift is a 'Have a great flight' item — bought in transit, utterly irrelevant to his arrival, the opposite of what anyone would want as a gift
jokes.explanation: A simple but well-executed visual gag. The irony of a 'have a great flight' gift given by someone who just arrived is a tight, economical joke. Alexis's warm reading of it ('so sweet') adds a layer of gentle absurdism.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I look like a Goldfish cracker.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You look like you've been slow-roasted.

jokes.text: 'Um, who's babysitting the turtles?'
jokes.setup: Ted and Alexis are having an emotional reunion after a long time apart
jokes.punchline: Alexis's first practical concern after they embrace is the endangered turtles Ted is supposed to be protecting in the Galapagos
jokes.explanation: Four words that reveal everything about how Alexis has grown: she's thinking practically about the work Ted cares about. But it's also funny because it cuts against the romantic moment. The specificity of 'turtles' as a concern is inherently charming and slightly absurd. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because you need it. I have Mediterranean heritage, Patrick. My skin is a delicate ecosystem.

jokes.text: "Ew Ted, they made you drink milk on the airplane?" "No I actually ordered it, and in my defense, they were serving cookies, so."
jokes.setup: Ted explains he got food poisoning from milk on the airplane
jokes.punchline: Alexis assumes he was forced to drink it; Ted admits he ordered it voluntarily, defending himself with the cookies rationale
jokes.explanation: Ted's guileless defense — ordering milk on an airplane because of cookies — is perfect character comedy. It's exactly the kind of wholesome, slightly dorky logic Ted operates on. Alexis's 'Ew' is a good straight-woman beat.

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: You look like a tangelo with a business degree.

jokes.text: "Yeah, yeah, no need to milk it."
jokes.setup: Alexis says she has 50 more questions about the milk poisoning story
jokes.punchline: Ted delivers a pun — 'no need to milk it' — immediately after telling a story about food poisoning from milk
jokes.explanation: The pun is groan-worthy but the timing and self-awareness make it work. It's a Ted-specific joke — he's just dorky enough to make a pun immediately after a milk story. Alexis's 'Ooh, we definitely don't have time for that' is a perfect beat on top.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I can't have you over to my place. My mother's been going through some things, and the last thing she needs is unexpected company.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Plus, every time someone visits, she takes it as an opportunity to show them her Zoom meetings from her book club. And let me tell you, those things really help me zoom out of there.

jokes.text: "Ooh, we definitely don't have time for that." (Alexis immediately shutting down Ted's milk pun)
jokes.setup: Ted makes a milk pun
jokes.punchline: Alexis's deadpan 'we definitely don't have time for that' dismisses both the pun AND implicitly the 50 milk questions, redirecting to intimacy
jokes.explanation: A tight reaction beat. The subtext (they have limited time together and she'd rather not spend it on puns) makes the dismissal funnier than it appears on paper.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I have something important I need to talk to you about.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, is this like breaking up with someone from the Backstreet Boys while they're on tour?

jokes.text: Johnny surveys the rundown motel room with nostalgia: 'untouched from the 60s' / 'boutique hotels would kill for this level of authenticity'
jokes.setup: Johnny and Roland are looking at the new motel property, which is clearly in poor shape
jokes.punchline: Johnny spins the dilapidation as a feature — 'authenticity' — the classic entrepreneur's delusion
jokes.explanation: A solid bit of Johnny Rose optimism/self-delusion. The joke is in the gap between what he says and what Roland (and the audience) see. Functional but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I just don't respond well to pressure. Like, remember when I made that decision about my hair for prom? I got it all chopped off and highlighted the same day, and I looked like a angry bird.

jokes.text: "I'm reminded John of Shanghai and our Party Secretary Suite with its own aquarium, I'd lay in a claw foot tub watching the orcas."
jokes.setup: Johnny is inspecting the motel room's potential as a Presidential Suite
jokes.punchline: Moira casually references laying in a claw foot tub watching captive orcas in Shanghai — an absurdly specific and opulent memory deployed in comparison to a budget motel
jokes.explanation: A beautifully calibrated Moira line. The specificity of 'Party Secretary Suite' (already funny), compounded by watching orcas in a claw foot tub, creates an image so specific and lavish it can only be Moira Rose. 'Or freed' payoff makes it even better.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Is this one of those things where if you touch them both at the same time, you'll be electrocuted?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Because I once knew a woman in Marrakech who did that, and she was never quite the same.

jokes.text: 'Joc.' / 'And we're back.'
jokes.setup: Moira has drifted into a reverie about Shanghai orcas; Roland appears to call her back
jokes.punchline: Roland calls out 'Joc' — short for Jocelyn his wife — apparently mixing the two women up while bringing Moira back to reality; Johnny's 'And we're back' is a perfectly weary reaction to Moira's tangents
jokes.explanation: Johnny's 'And we're back' is a beautifully exhausted shorthand for years of managing Moira's flights of fancy. A small but efficient joke.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's like parting the Red Sea, except the sea is my hips and the Egyptians are my thighs.

jokes.text: "Well, I can almost hear the orcas calling to be fed... or freed."
jokes.setup: Johnny closes his eyes to imagine the luxury Moira described, picturing orcas in a private aquarium
jokes.punchline: He adds 'or freed' — a quiet acknowledgment of the moral horror of captive orcas, delivered deadpan
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The addition of 'or freed' is perfect — it's a tiny ethical interjection delivered with complete calm, subverting the nostalgic reverie. The economy is exceptional: two words do all the work. It's the kind of joke you might miss on first watch.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Have we managed fine in this room?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Fine? We're living like crustations in a dumpster.

jokes.text: Moira has already seen the movie 'Miranda Rights' three times but Roland treats it like a neutral fact
jokes.setup: Moira mentions Johnny made her buy the Blu-ray of her own feminist police drama
jokes.punchline: Roland reveals he rented it three times, treating Moira's vanity project with total nonchalance
jokes.explanation: The joke is mainly that Roland has seen Moira's niche feminist drama three times without irony. Solid character beat, moderately funny.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: These photos are good.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You only picked flattering ones of yourself.

jokes.text: "Miranda is so sassy when she went undercover for that wet T-shirt contest."
jokes.setup: Roland mentions he's seen Moira's feminist drama 'Miranda Rights' three times
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn enthusiastically describes the plot — specifically that it involves a wet T-shirt contest undercover scene, which is antithetical to the show's described 'gritty feminist' premise
jokes.explanation: This is a gem. The contrast between 'gritty feminist police drama' and 'undercover wet T-shirt contest' is perfectly absurdist. Jocelyn's genuine enthusiasm makes it funnier — she sees no contradiction. Strong originality, tight structure.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Do you want to go back for more spray tanning?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Oh yes, I would love to go back and have a stranger spray me down with a toxic orange mist. That sounds like a dream.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: No. I was being sarcastic.

jokes.text: "Bob has his first cry therapy session tonight."
jokes.setup: Jocelyn mentions they need to get home because Bob is babysitting
jokes.punchline: She mentions, as a throwaway detail, that Bob has his first cry therapy session tonight — an absurd concept dropped with total normalcy
jokes.explanation: The joke is entirely in the casualness of the delivery. 'Cry therapy' as a concept is already funny; that Bob has a 'first session' implies he's committed to a course of treatment. The throwaway nature is key — it's not explained or dwelt on.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: If you really want to send Patrick's relatives something, I have some old beauty pageant photos from when I was younger. Those should do the trick.

jokes.text: "Well, if that isn't teamwork, I don't know what is." / "Oh, I'm talking about the way you and I threw them off the fragrance so we could keep this suite to ourselves."
jokes.setup: Johnny congratulates himself on getting the rooms clean; Moira agrees but reveals she was scheming to keep the suite
jokes.punchline: Moira used a theatrical metaphor ('threw them off the fragrance') for what was a deceptive maneuver, while Johnny thought they were genuinely doing the right thing
jokes.explanation: 'Threw them off the fragrance' is a very Moira malapropism — 'scent' would be the standard phrase. Her casual revelation that she's been scheming while Johnny was earnest is a great character dynamic moment.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Alexis, I know this might not be the best time, but I've been wanting to tell you... I think you're really special. I mean, I've always thought you were special, but lately I've been thinking about it more, and I realized that life is short and you never know what's going to happen, so I thought maybe I should just say something before it's too late.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ted... I'm literally in the middle of a personal crisis right now.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I know, I know. That's actually why I thought now would be a good time. You know, to remind you that there are good things in life?

jokes.text: "Oh, be careful John, lest you suffer vertigo from the dizzying heights of your moral ground."
jokes.setup: Johnny insists he wasn't scheming to keep the suite and has the moral high ground
jokes.punchline: Moira delivers a grandiloquent warning about Johnny's moral heights — 'dizzying heights of your moral ground' is an elaborate, theatrical way to call him self-righteous
jokes.explanation: A quintessential Moira line — the elaborate, theatrical construction of a fairly simple point (you're being self-righteous) is her defining comedic mode. 'Dizzying heights of your moral ground' is a beautiful coinage. Highly quotable, perfectly Moira. Justifies 8+ score.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, I could just leave everything and follow Ted to an island.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're not going to follow Ted to an island.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I followed Harry Styles to Coachella.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That was one weekend, and you came back because it was too hot.

jokes.text: Ray's backdrop options for the engagement shoot: jungle, outer space, two separate volcanoes
jokes.setup: David and Patrick need engagement photos for their wedding
jokes.punchline: Ray the photographer's backdrop portfolio is entirely theme-park absurd: jungle, outer space, volcanoes
jokes.explanation: Ray's amateur photographer absurdity is a reliable gag. The specificity of showing TWO volcano options is a nice escalation detail.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: You know, Alexis, sometimes when you're facing a really difficult decision, it helps to remember that whatever you choose, you'll eventually be okay with it.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Eventually?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Well, yes. Eventually.

jokes.text: David has draped everything in Ray's studio with white tarps and calls it 'an English estate in the offseason'
jokes.setup: The previous backdrop (volcano etc.) was rejected; David has taken over art direction
jokes.punchline: David's 'sophisticated' alternative is literally just covering everything in tarps, which Patrick accurately describes as looking like 'someone put a tarp over their living room furniture'
jokes.explanation: The visual gag (inferred from dialogue) of David's 'artful' setup being a bunch of tarps is a great joke. Patrick's practical reading ('someone put a tarp over their living room furniture') vs. David's aspirational framing ('English estate in the offseason') is a perfect distillation of their dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Are you alright?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm fine. I was just doing my Kennedy impression.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, it was devastating.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Thank you.

jokes.text: Ray offers to show the 'county fair backdrop' because 'a Ferris wheel would look so cute on a mouse pad'
jokes.setup: Patrick has just objected to David's tarp setup as a backdrop
jokes.punchline: Ray reads the room completely wrong and pivots back to mouse pads with a county fair option, not understanding that mouse pads are off the table
jokes.explanation: The third mouse pad callback escalates the running gag nicely. Ray's inability to read the room combined with his fixation on mouse pads as a product category is well-established now and pays off.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That was not a JFK impression, that was a cry for help. And darling, if you're going to attempt an accent, at least commit to the physicality. I once played a Portuguese widow in a Soho experimental theater piece, and I didn't just change my voice — I changed my entire skeletal structure.

jokes.text: 'I don't think we're gonna do the mouse pads.'
jokes.setup: Ray has once again pitched mouse pads
jokes.punchline: David's restrained, weary response is the comedic equivalent of 'please stop'
jokes.explanation: The joke is entirely in David's controlled, definitive delivery of a sentence that should be unnecessary to say. The gap between what needs to be said and the patience required to say it calmly is the comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I see you two are really putting those cleaning supplies to good use.

jokes.text: 'No, I just put mine on your credit card.'
jokes.setup: David asks if Patrick gave Stevie his spray tan appointment
jokes.punchline: Stevie reveals she simply charged her own spray tan to David's credit card — no permission requested
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect economy — eight words that are both a confession and completely unapologetic. Stevie's utter lack of guilt is the joke. The beat where David has to process that Stevie committed credit card fraud against him and she's completely unfazed is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: We could swap houses for the weekend.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, you stay at my place, I stay at yours. With our families.

jokes.text: Patrick emerges from the spa with an extreme orange spray tan; "You look like a cheese puff."
jokes.setup: Patrick went to get a subtle spray tan; David told him to get 'what David usually gets'
jokes.punchline: Patrick emerges visibly orange; David's perfectly calibrated insult is 'you look like a cheese puff'
jokes.explanation: "You look like a cheese puff" is one of the episode's most quotable lines and the biggest pure comedy beat of the spray tan subplot. Patrick pre-empting David with 'I look like a cheese puff' — saying it himself — is a sharp structural choice that makes it even better. High impact, highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Ask not what your country can do for you, ask what you can do for your country. That's JFK.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That's not even close to what he said.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's exactly what he said. I do a very accurate JFK impression.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Your JFK sounds like a man having a stroke.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, he did have back problems, so maybe that's historically accurate.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That's not how any of this works.

jokes.text: "I told them to give me what you usually get." "Well, why would you do that? I'm practically Sicilian, you don't need that much sun."
jokes.setup: Patrick explains how he ended up orange
jokes.punchline: David's explanation — 'I'm practically Sicilian' — is both absurdly self-aggrandizing and a genuine attempt to explain skin tone differences
jokes.explanation: 'I'm practically Sicilian' is a perfectly David Rose statement — a self-mythologizing claim about his own Mediterranean heritage that is both specific and impossible to verify. The 'you don't need that much sun' is delivered with genuine concern that makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I was very firm with them.

jokes.text: "Oh hi Patrick, I almost didn't recognize you, you look like one of those people who has that skin condition from eating one too many carrots."
jokes.setup: Patrick walks in with his extreme spray tan
jokes.punchline: Stevie describes his color as a medical condition from eating carrots — carotenemia — with complete clinical detachment
jokes.explanation: Stevie's deadpan pivot from pleasantry to medical diagnosis is perfect. The joke works because she's not being mean — she genuinely seems to be offering a possible explanation. 'One too many carrots' is a nice understatement for what is clearly an extreme spray tan situation.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Ted, you're not looking good. Here, I made you some mac and cheese, and a nice creamy soup, and some ice cream.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Alexis, I think I'm lactose intolerant.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, why didn't you say something?!

jokes.text: "My mom's staying at my place and I'd rather not walk in on her and her zumba instructor, don't wanna have to zoom back here."
jokes.setup: Alexis suggests going to Ted's place; Ted explains why that's not ideal
jokes.punchline: Ted implies his mother is having an affair with her zumba instructor, then makes a 'zoom/zumba' pun — a terrible pun that is perfectly Ted
jokes.explanation: The zumba/zoom pun is deliberately groan-worthy — it's a Ted joke, intentionally dorky. The dark backstory (mom/zumba instructor) earns a laugh before the pun even lands. Well-integrated into character.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: There's an elephant in the room.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, there's literally an elephant in the room. I don't know how it got here, and I certainly don't know how to take care of it.

jokes.text: "You're starting to sound like me trying to end things with B-Rock on the Backstreet Boys Millennium Tour."
jokes.setup: Ted's serious tone makes Alexis think he's about to break up with her
jokes.punchline: Alexis's frame of reference for relationship endings is a specific Backstreet Boys tour — 'B-Rock' is AJ McLean's nickname, and the specificity of 'Millennium Tour' makes it feel genuinely autobiographical
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best character comedy moments. The specificity of 'B-Rock on the Backstreet Boys Millennium Tour' is magnificent — it implies Alexis had a real relationship with a Backstreet Boy, which is exactly the kind of insane backstory she would have. The joke works on multiple levels: it's funny as a non-sequitur, funny as Alexis characterization, and funny because she's using it to contextualize her current situation. Justifies 8+ easily.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Actually, they're not gross little flies. They're fruit flies, and they're a cornerstone of modern genetics research. Decades of scientific advancement wouldn't be possible without them.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: So... does that mean you'd like to help me with my research?

jokes.text: "You know that I can't make big decisions under pressure, that's how I ended up with a pixie cut at prom."
jokes.setup: Ted has revealed the serious relationship dilemma and Alexis needs time to process
jokes.punchline: Alexis explains her decision-paralysis with the non-sequitur example of getting a pixie cut at prom — a deeply specific and trivial consequence to describe her limitations
jokes.explanation: Another strong Alexis characterization joke. The pixie cut at prom is the exact kind of glamour-adjacent disaster that would haunt Alexis. It's a perfect deflation of the dramatic moment — the episode's heaviest emotional beat punctured by this trivial but specific memory.

jokes.text: "I liked this a whole lot more before we started talking."
jokes.setup: The Ted/Alexis relationship dilemma has just reached its emotional climax
jokes.punchline: Alexis cuts through all the emotion with the most relatable, honest, and slightly shallow observation about the situation
jokes.explanation: Beautifully timed line at the end of a heavy emotional scene. The humor works because it's emotionally true — she really did like it more when they were just being together — but it's framed with Alexis's characteristic breeziness. The economy is near-perfect. A great example of character comedy timing.

jokes.text: Moira puts her hands in freezing water to test if it's warming up — refuses to move them because 'how will I know if it's getting warmer if I'm not touching it?'
jokes.setup: Moira is running a bath in the Presidential Suite but the water is freezing
jokes.punchline: Her solution to testing water temperature is to leave her hands in the cold water continuously — a perfectly illogical approach delivered as obvious wisdom
jokes.explanation: A lovely Moira moment — she applies theatrical logic to a basic plumbing problem. The 'how will I know if it's getting warmer if I'm not touching it' argument is delivered as though it's unassailable. Good character integration.

jokes.text: "Well, I'm sorry I'm not an alchemist, John, and it's not like there's a his sink for when the hers is inoperable."
jokes.setup: Johnny tells Moira to turn on the hot tap to balance the cold water
jokes.punchline: Moira calls adjusting water temperature 'alchemy' and complains that there isn't a designated 'his' sink for when hers is 'inoperable' — implying she's used to having his-and-hers sinks at minimum
jokes.explanation: Calling tap adjustment 'alchemy' is a great Moira word choice — it implies she has no framework for understanding plumbing. The his-and-hers sink complaint is perfect character work: she's not complaining about a cold tap, she's lamenting the absence of luxury. Highly character-specific.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I'm sorry I'm not an alchemist, John, and it's not like there's a his sink for when the hers is inoperable.'
jokes.setup: Johnny explains she needs to balance hot and cold taps — an extremely basic plumbing operation.
jokes.punchline: Moira frames mixing hot and cold water as 'alchemy' and reveals she considers separate his-and-hers sinks a reasonable minimum standard for a functioning bathroom.
jokes.explanation: The 'alchemist' line is spectacular — calling basic tap water temperature management 'alchemy' reveals the depth of Moira's domestic helplessness. The 'his and hers sink' detail is equally good: she considers this a basic amenity, not a luxury. Both elements together paint a portrait of a woman who has never used an ordinary bathroom. Strong character work.

jokes.text: Moira trying to squeeze through the narrow bathroom doorframe: "these door frames, it's like trying to force oneself through the eye of a needle."
jokes.setup: Johnny is navigating the cramped motel bathroom
jokes.punchline: Moira dramatically compares squeezing through a standard doorframe to passing through a needle's eye — biblical-scale suffering applied to a routine inconvenience
jokes.explanation: The biblical register of 'eye of a needle' applied to a standard motel bathroom doorframe is quintessential Moira dramatics. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Johnny thinks Moira is angling for a 'romantic weekend'; she clarifies she wants to upgrade their room permanently 'until they find a way out'
jokes.setup: Moira has been complaining about the motel bathroom; Johnny thinks she's building toward a romantic moment
jokes.punchline: She's actually been strategically manufacturing a case to upgrade their living situation — the 'romance' framing was a manipulation tactic
jokes.explanation: The misdirection of Johnny thinking this was romantic is well-set-up. Moira's strategic scheming is consistent with her character. Competent but not exceptional.

jokes.text: "Yes, in the same way road crustations are known to make due in old soda cans."
jokes.setup: Johnny asks if they haven't managed to make do in their regular motel room over the past few years
jokes.punchline: Moira agrees — but compares their existence to 'road crustations' (crustaceans) living in soda cans, a grotesque and specific image
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's very best lines. 'Road crustations' (her malapropism for crustaceans) in 'old soda cans' is a wonderfully specific and grotesque image for their living situation. The word 'crustations' alone is a great Moira-ism. The image earns a genuine laugh — it's original, character-perfect, and delivered with complete sincerity. Justifies 8+ easily.

jokes.text: 'They're practically middle-aged and they're not denying themselves betterment, why should we?'
jokes.setup: Johnny asks about abandoning the kids if they move to the Presidential Suite
jokes.punchline: Moira's defense is that David and Alexis are 'practically middle-aged' (they're in their late 20s-30s) and self-sufficient, and frames this as solidarity with their self-improvement
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: (1) Moira considering her adult children 'practically middle-aged' when she's clearly much older, and (2) framing abandoning her children in a motel room as a form of parental role-modeling. Classic Moira rationalization.

jokes.text: "Well, I've packed your weekender, it's in the car." (Moira has pre-packed before Johnny agreed)
jokes.setup: Johnny has just now agreed to stay in the suite as a business trial
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately reveals she already packed their bags before Johnny agreed — she knew she'd win
jokes.explanation: The pre-packed bag reveal is a perfect structural joke — the audience has just watched Johnny go through the deliberation process when Moira already knew the outcome. Economy is excellent; the beat lands clean.

jokes.text: "Those are just ones where you look good." "Well..."
jokes.setup: David is reviewing the engagement photo proofs and selecting the good ones
jokes.punchline: Patrick calls out that David has only selected photos where David himself looks good — and David's 'Well...' is a barely-guilty non-denial
jokes.explanation: David's 'Well...' non-denial is a perfect character beat. The joke is as much in what David doesn't say as what he does. Good economy.

jokes.text: "So I guess my relatives will just have to imagine what the person I'm marrying looks like and how happy I am to be with him."
jokes.setup: There are no usable photos where Patrick's face and hands are visible due to the spray tan
jokes.punchline: Patrick delivers a perfectly calibrated sarcastic summary of the situation — his relatives won't know what he looks like or that he's happy
jokes.explanation: Patrick's dry delivery of a genuinely absurd situation is well-executed. The detail that his 'face and hands' are the problem gives it specificity.

jokes.text: Stevie suggests opening the guest list or mailing out David's old pageant headshots to relatives instead of engagement photos
jokes.setup: There are no usable engagement photos; David worries his relatives won't know what Patrick looks like
jokes.punchline: Stevie's solutions are to either expand the guest list (so they can see Patrick in person) or mail out David's pageant headshots — neither of which addresses the actual problem
jokes.explanation: The pageant headshots suggestion is a nice absurdist escalation — mailing out David's personal glamour shots to relatives in lieu of engagement photos. Good Stevie irreverence.

jokes.text: 'David, can we just agree to stop making this wedding a bigger deal than it needs to be. We are not these people.'
jokes.setup: David and Alexis are escalating wedding planning chaos (expanding the guest list, headshots, etc.)
jokes.punchline: Patrick's plea to simplify — 'we are not these people' — is both genuine and ironic, given that all evidence suggests David absolutely IS 'these people'
jokes.explanation: The gentle irony of Patrick's denial ('we are not these people') while surrounded by mouse pads, volcanoes, and pageant headshots is a good beat. Patrick's intermittent denial of how chaotic his life has become is consistent character work.

jokes.text: Twyla offers Alexis a smoothie as solution to her existential crisis; Alexis responds: "No, I think I'll just take a smoothie."
jokes.setup: Twyla asks if a smoothie might help with Alexis's emotional distress
jokes.punchline: Alexis rejects the premise ('help') while accepting the smoothie itself — a small but precise distinction
jokes.explanation: A perfectly economical Alexis line. The distinction between 'a smoothie helping' and 'just taking a smoothie' is subtle but sharp — she refuses the emotional support framing while accepting the product. Good character integration.

jokes.text: "Do I leave everything behind and move to some random island to be with the love of my life? 'Cause I did that with Harry Styles in England, it was like too rainy."
jokes.setup: Alexis is weighing whether to follow Ted to the Galapagos
jokes.punchline: She frames it as a familiar situation — she's done this before with Harry Styles — and her deal-breaker was the weather
jokes.explanation: The episode's highest-impact joke. The Harry Styles reference is already great, but 'it was like too rainy' as the reason she left England — not the relationship itself, but the weather — is a devastating character reveal. It's absurd, it's completely Alexis, and it's delivered to Twyla as a perfectly normal comparison. The Backstreet Boys joke earlier set up Alexis's celebrity romance history; this escalates it brilliantly. Highest quotability in the episode. Clearly justifies 8.6 craft and 9.0 impact.

jokes.text: 'Do I like move to the Galapagos? Or does he move back here and turn down his dream job... Okay this just got like very heavy.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is explaining Ted's situation to Twyla
jokes.punchline: Alexis processes the impossible situation and lands on 'this just got very heavy' as her emotional summary — delivered with the same energy she'd use to describe a bad hair day
jokes.explanation: Alexis's understatement of a genuinely heartbreaking situation is classic character writing. The gap between the weight of the situation and her delivery is the joke.

jokes.text: "I know you, Alexis, and you always make the right decision." [beat] "Eventually."
jokes.setup: Twyla is trying to be supportively encouraging to Alexis
jokes.punchline: The qualifier 'Eventually' — added after a pause — undercuts the compliment perfectly, acknowledged that Alexis's track record is mixed
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured two-beat joke. The warmth of 'you always make the right decision' followed by the honest pause and 'Eventually' is structurally perfect. Twyla's kindness and her inability to fully commit to a compliment she knows isn't quite true is great character work in very few words.

jokes.text: Johnny does a JFK impersonation: "If not now, when? If not us, who?" — Moira: "Best to leave the voice work to me when setting a mood."
jokes.setup: Johnny is playing into the Presidential Suite theme with a JFK impression
jokes.punchline: Moira — a professional actress — dismisses his attempt with the polite but devastating 'best to leave the voice work to me when setting a mood'
jokes.explanation: Moira's professional-actress dismissal of Johnny's impression is great character comedy — she's the expert and can't help asserting it even romantically. 'When setting a mood' makes it funnier — as if this were a specific performance context requiring a specialist.

jokes.text: Roland and Jocelyn arrive at the Presidential Suite — catching Johnny and Moira with martinis in the room they were told they couldn't use
jokes.setup: Johnny lectured Roland earlier about not treating business assets as personal possessions; Moira scheemed to keep the suite
jokes.punchline: Roland and Jocelyn arrive to find Johnny and Moira doing exactly what Johnny forbade Roland from doing
jokes.explanation: A well-earned structural callback. The episode has been building to this hypocrisy reveal — Johnny's moral authority collapsing at the exact moment he needs it most. The martinis as visual proof add to the cringe comedy.

jokes.text: "Oh, I guess martinis work better than wax, huh?"
jokes.setup: Johnny claims he and Moira were 'doing a final polish on the room'
jokes.punchline: Roland's martini observation punctures Johnny's flimsy excuse with perfect economy
jokes.explanation: Roland's best line of the episode. The phrasing is sharp — 'martinis work better than wax' — it's a specific, precise skewering of Johnny's lie delivered with total calm. The economy is excellent: nine words do a lot of work. This is Roland at his most withering without trying to be.

jokes.text: "Johnny, wasn't it you who said it was dangerous to treat your assets like personal possessions?"
jokes.setup: Roland has caught Johnny red-handed staying in the suite he denied Roland access to
jokes.punchline: Roland quotes Johnny's own words back to him with mild, deliberate irony
jokes.explanation: A strong structural callback — Roland has been set up as bumbling, but here he lands a clean, deserved gotcha. The role reversal is satisfying.

jokes.text: Roland reveals they've been renting out their extra bedroom to pay for the motel — a stranger has been making himself at home in Jocelyn's kitchen
jokes.setup: Roland explains why they wanted to use the Presidential Suite for the weekend
jokes.punchline: The revelation that a stranger has been living in their home — in Jocelyn's kitchen specifically — is a dark and funny piece of character backstory delivered as a normal hardship
jokes.explanation: A good comedic reveal that adds sympathy to Roland and Jocelyn while being darkly funny. 'A total stranger making themselves at home in my kitchen' is an effective cringe detail.

jokes.text: "Or you know what, you could give us the keys to your place — it would be like wife swap except we wouldn't be swapping wives, we'd be swapping mattresses."
jokes.setup: Roland suggests an alternative to sleeping in the truck with the baby
jokes.punchline: Roland's 'wife swap but for mattresses' concept is a strange non-sequitur that reveals Roland doesn't fully understand what makes Wife Swap interesting
jokes.explanation: Roland's Wife Swap comparison is charming and character-appropriate. The clarification that they'd be swapping mattresses (not wives) is a nice escalation of his obliviousness.

jokes.text: 'Or you know what, you could give us the keys to your place, it would be like wife swap except we wouldn't be swapping wives, we'd be swapping mattresses.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is about to let Roland have the Presidential Suite as a compromise
jokes.punchline: Roland counter-proposes a 'wife swap' but clarifies it would be a 'mattress swap' — then Johnny immediately offers the room
jokes.explanation: Roland's explanation of his own metaphor — clarifying that the wives are not being swapped — is funnier than the original idea would have been. The clarification implies he knew the original phrasing was alarming. His self-awareness about the phrase while still using it is very Roland.

jokes.text: Johnny immediately says 'you can take the room' the moment Roland mentions 'wife swap'
jokes.setup: Roland says 'it would be like wife swap'
jokes.punchline: Before Roland can finish his clarification, Johnny immediately and definitively offers the room — the mention of 'wife swap' was enough
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the timing — Johnny cuts off Roland's explanation to hand over the room without hearing the rest. The implication is that ANY scenario involving 'wife swap' and Roland is something Johnny wants no part of. Great reaction beat with excellent economy.

jokes.text: Confused JFK quotation argument — Johnny insists 'Ask not what your country can do for you' is JFK but Roland and Jocelyn argue it's FDR and that JFK is from Boston not Upstate New York
jokes.setup: Johnny has been doing JFK impressions throughout the scene; Roland and Jocelyn are skeptical
jokes.punchline: A confused four-way argument about whether FDR or JFK said the famous quote — and where JFK is from — plays out as everyone is both wrong and emphatic
jokes.explanation: A fun bit of ensemble confusion. The irony is that everyone is confident and mostly wrong. The specific geography argument (Boston vs. Upstate New York) adds a nice layer. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Johnny describes the suite as reminding him of 'the suites in Cuba before Castro took over' and keeps doing his JFK impression
jokes.setup: Roland and Jocelyn are about to enjoy the suite; Johnny is trying to be magnanimous
jokes.punchline: Johnny contextualizes a Schitt's Creek motel suite with pre-revolutionary Cuba, while still insisting on the JFK voice
jokes.explanation: Johnny's continued JFK bit is wearing thin but the Cuba reference adds a mild absurdist layer — comparing a rundown motel to pre-Castro Cuba is a funny comparison for Johnny Rose to make.

jokes.text: Patrick has closed the entire café and set up an elaborate dinner for Alexis — mozzarella sticks, four-cheese lasagne, blueberry cheesecake
jokes.setup: Alexis has been blindsided by Ted's job offer and is processing it
jokes.punchline: Patrick has responded by closing the café and preparing a menu of exclusively dairy-based comfort foods — all of Alexis's 'cheat day favourites'
jokes.explanation: The all-dairy menu is a charming visual gag. The callback to the milk poisoning is implicit. Patrick's gesture is warm and slightly absurd — the café being closed for her specifically is a sweet comedy beat.

jokes.text: "That's a lot of dairy."
jokes.setup: Patrick reveals the menu: mozzarella sticks, four-cheese lasagne, blueberry cheesecake
jokes.punchline: Alexis's only response to this elaborate gesture is a flat observation about the dairy content — an echo of the milk food poisoning incident
jokes.explanation: Three words that do perfect double duty: they're an Alexis-style flat observation, they acknowledge Patrick's effort with a slightly anticlimactic response, AND they callback to the milk storyline. Exceptional economy. The callback makes it funnier on rewatch.

jokes.text: "I may have forgotten about the whole milk situation."
jokes.setup: Alexis notes the all-dairy menu is 'a lot of dairy'
jokes.punchline: Patrick admits he forgot that Ted got food poisoning from milk — making his well-intentioned comfort food menu accidentally ironic
jokes.explanation: A satisfying callback resolution — Patrick's inadvertent dairy menu after a milk poisoning is a good structural joke. 'I may have forgotten' is understated and perfect.

jokes.text: 'Huh, that's a lot of dairy.'
jokes.setup: Alexis has revealed the set menu: mozzarella sticks, four cheese lasagne, cheesecake
jokes.punchline: Ted's understated observation that a menu designed for him on the night after his milk food poisoning is extremely dairy-heavy
jokes.explanation: Five words doing maximum work. Ted's gentle, non-accusatory observation is perfect — he's immediately clocked the dairy situation, remembers the milk poisoning, but is touched enough by the gesture that he doesn't complain. The economy is exceptional; the character integration is high. A quiet standout.

jokes.text: "And just think of all those gross little flies that are still out there for you to discover."
jokes.setup: Alexis is telling Ted to pursue his dream job in the Galapagos
jokes.punchline: Alexis's encouragement is delivered in her own vernacular — 'gross little flies' for the scientifically invaluable Galapagos insects — which undercuts the gravity of the moment with her genuine but limited scientific vocabulary
jokes.explanation: A lovely blend of comedy and character growth. Alexis is genuinely being supportive, but her description of scientifically invaluable insects as 'gross little flies' is both funny and revealing. Ted's earnest scientific correction adds a layer.

jokes.text: "I'm sure there'll be like some other woman, somewhere."
jokes.setup: Ted says he doesn't think he'll ever meet another woman who makes him feel the way Alexis does — a romantic declaration
jokes.punchline: Alexis responds to this declaration with 'I'm sure there'll be like some other woman, somewhere' — a completely flat, slightly dismissive acknowledgment that is both funny and a little heartbreaking
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest moments — a joke that works on multiple levels simultaneously. The flat delivery undercuts a romantic moment, but it also reveals Alexis has grown: she's not clinging, she's being genuinely selfless. 'Like some other woman, somewhere' is the perfect Alexis construction — the 'like' and vague 'somewhere' making it feel both dismissive and quietly devastated. Exceptional character integration and economy. Justifies high scores.

jokes.text: 'I'm sure there'll be like some other woman, somewhere.'
jokes.setup: Ted has just said he doesn't think he'll ever meet another woman who made him feel the way Alexis does
jokes.punchline: Alexis's response to this profound romantic declaration is to mildly suggest that statistically, another woman probably exists somewhere
jokes.explanation: A perfect final comedic beat in a heartbreaking scene. Alexis's inability to fully receive a romantic statement — deflecting with statistical pragmatism — is both funny and characteristic. The halting delivery ('like some other woman, somewhere') suggests she's processing her own grief while trying to be reassuring, and the humor emerges from that conflict. It's bittersweet comedy at its best: the joke exists because Alexis can't quite hold the moment without deflecting. High character integration, excellent structure, very quotable.

tmdb_overview: Johnny and Moira spend a night at the presidential suite in the new motel, while Alexis receives a surprise visitor.
insight: Wordplay and absurdist humor sustain a quieter finale despite below-average impact scores.
